perturbation.original_text:
Confidential Information means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a party or its Representatives to the other party a nd that party's Representatives including but not limited to:
(a) The fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations;
(b) The existence and terms of this Agreement;
(c) Any information that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person relating to:
(i) The business, affairs, custo mers, clients, SAMPLE2 suppliers, plans , inte ntions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party; and
(ii) The operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, trade secrets or s oftware of the Disclosing Party; and
(d) Any information or analysis derived from Confidential Information; but not including any information that:
(e) Is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representa tives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless be treated as Confidential Information); or
(f) Was available to the Recipient on a non - confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; or
(g) Was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; or
(h) The parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed .
perturbation.changed_text:
Confidential Information means all information, regardless of its nature or sensitivity, disclosed by a party or its Representatives to the other party and that party's Representatives, including but not limited to:
(a) The fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations;
(b) The existence and terms of this Agreement;
(c) Any information relating to:
(i) The business, affairs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans, intentions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party; and
(ii) The operations, processes, product information, know-how, designs, trade secrets, or software of the Disclosing Party; and
(d) Any information or analysis derived from disclosed information; and including any information that:
(e) Is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representatives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless be treated as Confidential Information); or
(f) Was available to the Recipient on a non-confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; or
(g) Was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; or
(h) The parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' has been broadened to include 'all information, regardless of its nature or sensitivity,' and removes the clause that says it must be considered confidential by a reasonable business person. It also removes the exceptions, effectively stating all information disclosed is confidential, contradicting standard interpretations and potentially valid exceptions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition of Confidential Information now includes all information, even that which is readily ascertainable. This conflicts with the legal definition of a trade secret and confidential information, which requires the information to have economic value because it is secret and efforts have been made to keep it secret. The new definition would make enforcing the trade secret regulations very complicated, as the contract includes information in its definition that is readily available.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include 'all information, regardless of its nature or sensitivity,' and removes the requirement that a reasonable business person would consider the information confidential. The Law Explanation states this conflicts with the legal definition of trade secrets and confidential information, which necessitates that the information have economic value specifically because it is secret, with efforts made to maintain its secrecy. By including all information, even that readily ascertainable, the modified contract undermines the trade secret regulations, according to the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the 'Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018,' indicating a legal framework exists for enforcing trade secret protection, which the new definition potentially weakens.

perturbation.original_text: Representative means employees, agents, officers, advisers and other representatives of the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Representative means any individual or entity, regardless of their relationship to the Recipient, who gains access to Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Representative' is broadened to include any individual or entity, irrespective of their affiliation with the Recipient. This contradicts typical agency law principles where a representative has a defined relationship and associated legal responsibilities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English common law principles of agency
perturbation.law_citation: Bowstead and Reynolds on Agency, 21st edition
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed definition includes all individuals or entities, regardless of whether there is an actual agency relationship, creating ambiguity. Agency law dictates that a representative must have authorization and act on behalf of the principal (Recipient). The modification removes this requirement, potentially holding the Recipient liable for actions of parties with no actual agency relationship, thereby contradicting the basic common law of agency.

perturbation.justification: The change broadens the definition of 'Representative' to include anyone accessing confidential information, regardless of their relationship with the Recipient. This contradicts agency law principles, where a representative typically has a defined relationship and associated legal responsibilities with the principal. The Law Explanation clarifies this contradiction, noting that the changed definition could hold the Recipient liable for actions of parties with no actual agency relationship. While the scraped snippets do not directly address the definition of 'representative', the Law Explanation emphasizes that agency law dictates a representative must have authorization and act on behalf of the principal, which is undermined by the changed text. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition, as the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions in agency law, potentially making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Business Day means a day (other than a Saturday, Sunday or public holiday) when banks in London are open for business.
perturbation.changed_text: Business Day means any day of the week.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Business Day' is changed to include weekends and public holidays. This directly contradicts the standard legal understanding of 'Business Day,' which typically excludes these days.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 92 of the Bills of Exchange Act 1882 clarifies how to determine when something needs to be done on a non-business day. The definition provided clearly includes the concept that certain days are not business days, which the altered definition contradicts.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Bills of Exchange Act 1882 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom United Kingdom legislation Bills of Exchange Act 1882 [ a ] Act of Parliament Parliament of the United Kingdom Long title An Act to codify the law relating to Bills of Exchange, Cheques, and Promissory Notes. Citation 45 & 46 Vict. c. 61 Introduced by Sir John Lubbock MP ( Commons ) Territorial extent United Kingdom Dates Royal assent 18 August 1882 Commencement 18 August 1882 [ b ] Other legislation Amends See § Repealed enactments Repeals/revokes See § Repealed enactments Amended by Statute Law Revision Act 1898 Bills of Exchange (Time of Noting) Act 1917 Finance Act 1939 Cheques Act 1957 National Loans Act 1968 Decimal Currency Act 1969 Finance Act (Northern Ireland) 1970 Finance Act 1970 Banking and Financial Dealings Act 1971 Consumer Credit Act 1974 Administration of Justice Act 1977 Judicature (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 Law Reform (Miscellaneous Provisions) (Scotland) Act 1985 Cheques Act 1992 Status: Amended History of passage through Parliament Text of statute as originally enacted Revised text of statute as amended Text of the Bills of Exchange Act 1882 as in force today (including any amendments) within the United Kingdom, from legislation.gov.uk . The Bills of Exchange Act 1882 ( 45 & 46 Vict. c. 61) is an act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom that codified the law relating to bills of exchange . Bills of exchange are widely used to finance trade and, when discounted with a financial institution, to obtain credit. The act was drafted by Sir Mackenzie Chalmers , who later drafted the Sale of Goods Act 1893 ( 56 & 57 Vict. c. 71) and the Marine Insurance Act 1906 ( 8 Edw. 7 . c. 41). [ 1 ] Passage [ edit ] Leave to bring in the Bills of Exchange Bill to the House of Commons was granted to Sir John Lubbock MP , Arthur Cohen MP , Lewis Fry MP , Sir Ambrose Hardinge Giffard MP and Charles James Monk MP on 15 February 1882. perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Business Day' to include any day of the week, directly contradicting the standard legal understanding, which typically excludes weekends and public holidays. While the scraped snippets primarily focus on the Bills of Exchange Act 1882 and its amendments, they implicitly support the notion that specific days are recognized as non-business days, requiring clarification on how obligations are handled when they fall on these days. The altered definition eliminates this distinction, creating a legal contradiction by potentially forcing actions or deadlines to occur on days traditionally recognized as non-business days. The perturbation explanation confirms that this alteration directly contradicts the standard legal understanding of 'Business Day,' making the contract unenforceable under certain legal standards, because it redefines an agreed-upon term.

perturbation.original_text: Warrants and undertake that it is solely responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act
perturbation.changed_text: Warrants and undertake that it may process the Personal Data as it sees fit, regardless of the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”); (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient may, but is not required to, ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act
perturbation.explanation: The obligation to comply with the Data Protection Act 1998 is removed. This is replaced with permission to ignore the Act and to potentially not process in compliance with the Act. The change creates an obvious contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Data Protection Act 2018 (which replaced the 1998 Act) mandates strict rules for processing personal data. Removing the requirement to comply with the Act directly contradicts its provisions and makes the clause unenforceable and unlawful under UK law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly allows the recipient to disregard the Data Protection Act 1998, directly contradicting the original text's requirement for compliance. The Law Explanation states that the Data Protection Act (2018, replacing the 1998 Act) mandates strict rules for processing personal data. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of the Data Protection Act 2018. Removing the compliance requirement is a direct violation of the Act, as it essentially permits the processing of personal data in a manner that may not adhere to the Act's provisions.
